title: Developing a Uniface Application

Developing a Uniface Application

Developing a Uniface Application

Use the Uniface IDE to create development objects, and then build them into Uniface archive files that are immediately deployed into a standalone test environment.

Developing applications is an iterative process, so the following steps provide a high-level guide to the activities involved in developing a Uniface application.

  1. Create a project.

    The starting point for developing all applications is the Project. A project can represent a complete application, or a logical block of an application such as an authentication module.

    1. From the Start Page in the Uniface IDE, click Create Project. The new project is opened in the Project Editor, where you can create and edit its contents.
    2. Create new objects or add existing objects to the project using the Resource Browser.
    3. Optionally, and at any time, you can reorganize your development objects into functional sub-projects; for example, for distribution.
  2. Model the application.

    Once you have created Modeled Entities in the Project Editor, you can define their fields, keys, and relationships to capture the data structure, business rules, and default functionality of the application. As much as possible, define common and default behavior in the modeled objects, rather than in components.

    1. In the Entity Editor, you can add Fields and Keys to the Entity.
    2. If applicable, you can define Relationships with other Entities.
    3. In addition to Modeled Entities and their children, you can create libraries of modeled definitions and IncludeScript that can be used to speed development, enforce consistency, and make maintenance easier.
    4. Optionally, define component templates to use in creating components that share common behavior. For example, if you need to create a number of components for browsing different entities, a component template can make it easier, faster, and more consistent.
  3. Construct components and runtime libraries.

    Create the components needed for your application. In most cases, these will use the Modeled Entities that you have already created. You can also create runtime libraries to centralize common messages and functionality.

    1. In the Component Editor, use the worksheets to define the component structure, write ProcScript, and design the component layout.
    2. Create libraries of reusable objects for use at runtime, such as messages, global variables, and ProcScript.
    3. If applicable, integrate other third-part components, such as web services. Uniface enables you to integrate non-Uniface components into the application, providing both call-in and call-out support for a wide variety of third-party technologies, including web services.

      At a minimum, you will need to define signatures for third-party components.

    4. Optionally, construct an application shell. For web applications, a default application shell is provided by the Uniface Server.
  4. Build and test the application.
    1. To build the application, click the Build button in the Project Editor. During this process, development objects are compiled into runtime objects, which are packaged in a Uniface archive file. These are automatically made available to the standalone runtime environment used for testing.
    2. Test the application.

When the application is complete, it can be deployed and configured in the production environment.
